Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

3 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.

Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → Machine coding, testing, and follow-up handling

LLD learning path

Machine coding, testing, and follow-up handling

Rehearse how to present class design, write extensible code under time pressure, and adapt cleanly when the interviewer adds new requirements.

Module progress 0%

0 of 3 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Machine-coding skeletons and incremental delivery

Start with the thin vertical slice that proves the model, then add interfaces, policies, and edge cases in an order the interviewer can follow.

Why this module matters Design responsibilities cleanly before patterns start to stack up.

Move through the lessons in order so each object-model decision has context before follow-up requirements and machine-coding trade-offs appear.

Study target 3 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

Low-level design interview prep 3 lessons 0/3 complete Follow in order for best results Back to LLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the prompt shape, object boundaries, and extension seams before coding anything.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
LLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Structure machine-coding answers so core classes appear quickly
  • Design for testability, concurrency, and follow-up changes
  • Narrate trade-offs while keeping the implementation incremental

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core object or workflow decision in your own words.
  • Sketch the key classes, responsibilities, and extension seams before you code.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions, invariants, and object boundaries.
  • Justified abstraction choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of follow-ups such as testing, concurrency, and extensibility.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

Lesson 1 Next
Machine-coding skeletons and incremental delivery

Start with the thin vertical slice that proves the model, then add interfaces, policies, and edge cases in an order the interviewer can follow.

Testing seams, refactoring, and interview-safe cleanup

Use tests and small refactors to prove design quality while preserving momentum during a live round.

20-30 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Concurrency follow-ups and bridging into scale

Handle common interviewer pivots around thread safety, shared state, and how an object design changes once the system has to scale out.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Machine-coding skeletons and incremental delivery

Open

Start with the thin vertical slice that proves the model, then add interfaces, policies, and edge cases in an order the interviewer can follow.

Why it matters

A clean LLD interview is not only about the final class diagram. It is also about sequencing implementation choices so the interviewer sees judgment rather than rushed coding.

What you will cover

Open with the core sliceNarrate what comes nextLeave the design reviewable

Good interview signal

  • Implement one end-to-end happy path first.
  • State where extension points will land before over-abstracting.
20-30 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Testing seams, refactoring, and interview-safe cleanup

Open

Use tests and small refactors to prove design quality while preserving momentum during a live round.

Why it matters

Interviewers often evaluate whether you can recognize design pressure early and carve out seams before the implementation hardens into messy code.

What you will cover

Create seams for fast feedbackRefactor in small, visible stepsBalance delivery and polish

Good interview signal

  • Expose seams around external dependencies.
  • Test the rules that protect important invariants.
20-30 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Concurrency follow-ups and bridging into scale

Open

Handle common interviewer pivots around thread safety, shared state, and how an object design changes once the system has to scale out.

Why it matters

Strong candidates can explain not only the object model but also what breaks when multiple callers race, shared state grows hot, or the design must move from in-memory logic to distributed coordination.

What you will cover

Identify shared mutable statePrepare the design for scale transitionsAnswer follow-ups without overbuilding

Good interview signal

  • Point out shared mutable state and contention risks.
  • Explain what remains local logic versus distributed infrastructure.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
